title: Does Consumer Confidence Forecast Household Expenditure? A Sentiment Index Horse Race

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This 1998 Economic Policy Review article by New York Fed economists Jason Bram and Sydney Ludvigson compares the forecasting power of the University of Michigan Index of Consumer Sentiment versus the Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index for predicting household spending categories. The research found the Conference Board index has superior predictive power across most spending categories including total PCE, motor vehicles, services, and durable goods, while the Michigan index only helps forecast motor vehicles and goods excluding motor vehicles. The job prospects questions in the Conference Board survey were identified as having the most predictive power.
key_points: Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index outperformed University of Michigan Index in forecasting most household spending categories
Conference Board index significantly predicts: total personal consumption expenditures, motor vehicles, services, and durable goods excluding motor vehicles
Michigan index only helps forecast spending in two categories: motor vehicles and goods excluding motor vehicles
Two Conference Board questions about local job prospects exhibited the most predictive power among survey questions
Authors suggest policymakers should monitor consumer confidence surges driven by job availability questions as leading indicators for spending
Authors: Jason Bram and Sydney Ludvigson, New York Fed economists
why_it_matters: This research provides empirical guidance on which consumer sentiment survey to use for economic forecasting. For macro researchers and policymakers, the finding that Conference Board job prospects questions have superior predictive power suggests these specific survey responses may be more reliable leading indicators for consumer spending forecasts than the more widely-cited Michigan sentiment index.
macro_context: The research supports using Conference Board Consumer Confidence as a leading indicator for household spending, with specific emphasis on job prospects questions. This has implications for macro forecasting models and Fed assessments of consumer sector momentum.
